title: One Up On Wall Street
author: Peter Lynch with John Rothchild
subtitle: How to Use What You Already Know to Make Money in the Market
description: Legendary Fidelity Magellan Fund manager Peter Lynch explains how individual investors can outperform Wall Street professionals by investing in companies they already understand. The book introduces Lynch's six-category stock classification system, the PEG ratio, the 'tenbagger' concept, the two-minute drill, and why amateur investors have structural advantages over institutional money managers. Lynch argues that everyday observations — products you use, stores you shop at, industries you work in — are the best sources of investment ideas.
keyIdeas: Individual investors have structural advantages over Wall Street professionals
Six stock categories: Slow Growers, Stalwarts, Fast Growers, Cyclicals, Turnarounds, Asset Plays
The PEG ratio: P/E divided by earnings growth rate — fair value is PEG of 1.0
Tenbagger concept: stocks that return 10x your investment
The two-minute drill: explain your investment thesis in two minutes
Invest in what you know — everyday observations are the best source of stock ideas
Different stock categories have different buy, hold, and sell rules
Ignore macroeconomics and market predictions — focus on individual companies
The perfect stock: boring name, boring business, no analyst coverage, recurring revenue
Avoid hot stocks in hot industries, 'the next something,' and whisper stocks
keyTakeaways: You can beat Wall Street by investing in companies you already understand from daily life
Classify every stock into one of six categories — each has different risk, reward, and sell rules
The PEG ratio (P/E ÷ growth rate) tells you if a stock is fairly valued — aim for PEG under 1.0
A tenbagger (10x return) can transform your portfolio — look for small, profitable, underfollowed companies
You should be able to explain your investment thesis in two minutes — if you can't, you don't understand it
Ignore interest rates, GDP, and market forecasts — nobody can predict them
Sell when the story changes, not when the price moves
Great companies in boring industries outperform hot companies in exciting industries
Buy companies that buy back shares and have insiders buying
A portfolio of 5-10 well-researched stocks is better than 50 mediocre ones
whoShouldRead: Individual investors who want to pick their own stocks
Beginners looking for an accessible, practical introduction to stock investing
Experienced investors who want to systematize their stock research process
Anyone who thinks investing requires a Wall Street pedigree or finance degree
MBA students who want to understand how great fund managers actually think

relatedBooks.title: A Random Walk Down Wall Street
